topic: Two colleagues are both considered for the same promotion
raw_output:
[story 1]

Evelyn smoothed the lapel of her blazer for the tenth time, the fine wool suddenly feeling abrasive against her skin. Across the mahogany expanse of the conference table, Mark was nodding along to the VP’s strategy points, his smile wide and effortless. Evelyn watched the way his hand rested casually near his water glass, a picture of unearned confidence. Her palms were slick. She focused intently on the condensation tracing paths down her untouched glass of water, a small, futile attempt to anchor herself. Every time the VP addressed Mark, a small knot tightened in her chest, a dull, heavy pressure that made drawing a full breath feel like a conscious effort. *This is it,* she thought, the realization hitting her with a sudden, cold clarity. *The moment.*

[story 2]

"That’s a solid proposal, Mark," the manager praised, clapping him heartily on the shoulder. Mark returned the gesture with easy camaraderie, his eyes sparkling with genuine enthusiasm. Sarah stood near the window, her fingers drumming a rapid, silent rhythm against the cool glass of the frame. She kept glancing over at Mark, cataloging the subtle shifts in his posture—the slight lean forward, the way he held his chin just so. Her throat felt suddenly dry, constricted. When it was her turn to speak, the words seemed to snag in her mouth, rearranging themselves into meaningless fragments. She gripped the edge of her notepad so tightly her knuckles bleached white. She needed to appear composed, steady, but inside, a frantic energy was vibrating just beneath her skin, demanding release.

[story 3]

I replayed the meeting in my head, focusing on the precise cadence of the senior director’s voice. Every word felt weighted, significant, as if it were a physical object pressing down on my shoulders. When Mark presented his case, I found myself meticulously observing his breathing, trying to decode any vulnerability. My own hands felt oddly clumsy; I kept fiddling with the silver ring on my index finger, the metal cold against my skin. A slight tremor ran through my left hand, which I quickly tried to mask by resting it flat on the table. I wanted to speak up, to assert the depth of my experience, but the air seemed thick, refusing to allow any sound to escape. I just stared at the polished wood grain, waiting for the silence to break, praying it wouldn't.
